					<description><![CDATA[<p>Intel's expansion; Cadence AI super agent for PCB, advanced packaging; photonics fab wars; earlier Yongin fab; German funding; new CHIPS Act award; HBM standard; Quadric funding; AMS acquisition; earnings; Rapidus teams up.</p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/chip-industry-week-in-review-147/">Chip Industry Week In Review</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></description>
										<content:encoded><![CDATA[<h3><strong>Advanced manufacturing, packaging</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Intel Foundry </strong>will invest €5B to <a href="https://newsroom.intel.com/intel-foundry/intel-invests-5-billion-euro-to-expand-manufacturing-in-europe">expand Intel 3 capacity</a> at its Leixlip, Ireland campus. The company also entered high-volume manufacturing for a subset of Panther Lake processors manufactured on its 18A using <a href="https://www.asml.com/en/news/press-releases/2026/high-na-euv-reaches-new-readiness-milestone">ASML’s High-NA EUV technology</a>.</li>
<li><strong>UMC</strong> delivered the first production wafers for <strong>SILITH’s</strong> 1.6T <a href="https://www.umc.com/en/News/press_release/Content/technology_related/20260714">silicon photonics</a> platform from its 300mm Singapore fab. The announcement highlights high-volume manufacturing for optical-interconnect manufacturing and gives UMC a production base for its own silicon photonics platform, planned for customer development in 2027.</li>
<li><strong>Tower Semiconductor</strong> plans a roughly <a href="https://towersemi.com/2026/07/14/07142026/">$4B expansion</a> of silicon photonics, silicon-germanium, and advanced-packaging capacity in Japan.</li>
<li><strong>TSMC</strong> will invest an <a href="https://www.nist.gov/news-events/news/2026/07/trump-administration-secures-additional-100-billion-us-semiconductor">incremental $100B</a> in advanced semiconductor manufacturing and packaging facilities in Arizona, potentially expanding its footprint to about 12 U.S. fabs and packaging facilities.</li>
<li><strong>Samsung</strong> plans to begin operations at its first <a href="https://www.reuters.com/world/asia-pacific/samsung-electronics-move-up-start-chip-factory-yongin-2029-source-says-2026-07-13/">Yongin semiconductor fab by 2029</a>, speeding up the original schedule by one to two years.</li>
<li>Shanghai-based startup <strong>Yuanjiwei</strong> opened a new 8-inch pilot manufacturing line for 2D semiconductors, with plans to use the line for process development and small-volume production, as the company aims for <a href="https://www.scmp.com/tech/big-tech/article/3360208/chinese-chip-start-aims-build-5-nanometre-equivalent-chips-without-using-euv-2029">5nm-equivalent chips without EUV</a> by 2029.</li>
</ul>
<h3><strong>Government interventions</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>The European Commission</strong> approved <a href="https://ec.europa.eu/commission/presscorner/detail/en/ip_26_1585">€659M in German subsidies</a> for four new facilities producing silicon carbide epi-wafers, power MOSFETs, semiconductor metrology equipment, and specialized detector chips.</li>
<li><strong>The U.S. Commerce Department </strong>will provide <strong>Bosch</strong> <a href="https://www.nist.gov/news-events/news/2026/07/department-commerce-announces-direct-funding-agreement-bosch-225-million">CHIPS Act incentives</a> worth up to $225 million to help pay for a $2B conversion of its Roseville, California, facility into a silicon carbide chip fab.</li>
<li><strong>India’s government</strong> approved ~US$13.2B for <a href="https://www.pib.gov.in/PressReleasePage.aspx?PRID=2284784&amp;lang=1&amp;reg=48">Semicon 2.0</a>, covering chip design, equipment and materials, new fabs, advanced packaging, R&amp;D, and workforce development.</li>
</ul>
<h3><strong>Memory</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>JEDEC</strong> finalized a new memory standard, <a href="https://www.jedec.org/news/pressreleases/new-jedec%C2%AE-sphbm4-standard-enables-hbm4-class-bandwidth-organic-substrates">JESD330-4</a>, the SPHBM4 standard, which uses a narrower, faster interface to connect HBM4-class memory through organic package substrates rather than silicon interposers..</li>
<li><strong>Nanya Technology</strong> plans to invest about $16B in its <a href="https://www.nanya.com/Content/InvestorCalendar/5722_20260710_2Q26_Investor%20Conference_EG.pdf">new Taiwan DRAM fab —</a> up from the $9.4B originally planned — with the first phase expected to reach 30,000 wafer starts per month by 2028. The longer-term goal is 45,000 wafers per month.</li>
<li>Chinese DRAM maker <strong>CXMT</strong>’s $8.55B <a href="https://www.reuters.com/world/asia-pacific/cxmts-shanghai-ipo-more-than-200-times-oversubscribed-by-retail-investors-2026-07-16/">Shanghai IPO</a> was more than 200 times oversubscribed by retail investors.</li>
<li>Meanwhile, lawmakers urged the Commerce Department to <a href="https://chinaselectcommittee.house.gov/media/letters/moolenaar-whitesides-to-secretary-lutnick-hold-firm-on-chinese-memory-chips-ban">prohibit U.S. purchases of CXMT</a> and other Chinese memory companies.</li>
</ul>
<h3><strong>New Analysis</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li><a href="https://www.semi.org/en/semi-press-release/global-semiconductor-equipment-sales-forecast-to-reach-a-record-229-billion-dollars-in-2028-semi-reports">Global semiconductor equipment sales</a> will rise 23% to ~$166B in 2026 and reach a record ~$230B by 2028,  according to <strong>SEMI</strong>.</li>
<li><strong>Yole</strong> reports global <a href="https://www.yolegroup.com/press-release/mems-industry-ai-drives-a-growth-resurgence/">MEMS revenue</a> rose over 7% to $17B in 2025 and projects the market will grow to roughly $24B by 2031.</li>
<li><strong>Trendforce</strong> predicts <a href="https://www.trendforce.com/presscenter/news/20260713-13142.html">SLC NAND contract prices</a> will rise 120% to 170% in the second half of 2026.</li>
</ul>
<h3><strong>Where&#8217;s the funding going</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li><a href="https://semiengineering.com/startup-funding-q2-2026/">80 companies collectively raised over $6B</a> in the second quarter, with 18 companies reaching or exceeding $100M.</li>
<li><strong>Quadric</strong> extended its Series C to $46M to accelerate deployment of its <a href="https://quadric.ai/press-release/quadric-series-c-second-close-2026">programmable AI chip platform</a> across existing automotive, AI PC, and enterprise customers, while looking ahead to humanoid robotics, wearables, and networking.</li>
<li><strong>TYLsemi </strong>raised $43M for a full-stack <a href="https://www.tylsemi.ai/press-releases/tylsemi-raises-43-million-to-launch-first-full-stack-chipletplatform-for-custom-ai-silicon/">chiplet platform for custom AI</a> silicon, including I/O, power, and memory for XPUs.</li>
</ul>
<h3 role="heading" aria-level="2" data-uw-rm-heading="level"><strong>Notable deals</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li role="heading" aria-level="2" data-uw-rm-heading="level"><strong>Rapidus</strong> and <strong>Cadence</strong> are <a href="https://newsroom.cadence.com/press-releases/press-release-details/2026/Rapidus-and-Cadence-Partner-on-Agentic-AI-for-Advanced-SoC-Design/default.aspx">working to integrate</a> Cadence’s InnoStack AI Super Agent into Rapidus’ Raads chip design environment to automate parts of the SoC design flow from architecture through implementation and signoff. The companies aim to better align chip designs with Rapidus’  manufacturing process.</li>
<li role="heading" aria-level="2" data-uw-rm-heading="level"><strong>Diodes</strong> will <a href="https://www.diodes.com/about/news/press-releases/diodes-incorporated-to-acquire-elevate-semiconductor?utm_source=chatgpt.com">acquire</a> <strong>ElevATE Semiconductor</strong> for $250M in cash, adding mixed-signal ICs used primarily in semiconductor automated test equipment.</li>
<li role="heading" aria-level="2" data-uw-rm-heading="level"><strong>Arteris</strong> expanded its partnership with <strong>Arm</strong> to boost <a href="https://www.arteris.com/press-releases/arteris-expands-partnership-with-arm-to-accelerate-semiconductor-cybersecurity/">CPU cybersecurity assurance</a>, with Arm further integrating Arteris&#8217; Cycuity Radix technology into its security assurance processes.</li>
</ul>
<h3><strong>Research</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Imec</strong> and <strong>Diraq</strong> coherently operated and read out an <a href="https://www.imec-int.com/en/press/imec-and-diraq-demonstrate-first-coherent-operation-eight-silicon-mos-spin-qubits-fabricated">eight-qubit silicon MOS array</a> fabricated in a 300mm CMOS-compatible process, extending foundry-made spin qubits beyond previous one- and two-qubit demonstrations. The result strengthens the case that conventional semiconductor manufacturing can support larger quantum devices.</li>
</ul>
<h3><strong>Innovations</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>DFSX </strong>launched its DF1000 <a href="https://www.dfsx.com/products/ai-accelerator/df1000">high-performance AI accelerator</a>, which combines 3D DRAM near-memory computing with a SW-defined architecture, supporting large-model training and inference.</li>
<li><strong>Cadence</strong> launched its AuraStack AI <a href="https://newsroom.cadence.com/press-releases/press-release-details/2026/Cadence-Introduces-AuraStack-AI-Super-Agent-the-Worlds-First-Agentic-AI-Platform-for-PCB-and-Advanced-Packaging/default.aspx">Super Agent for PCB and advanced packaging</a> design, delivering up to 2X faster time to market and 15X higher productivity by coordinating domain-specific AI agents across planning, implementation, and multiphysics analysis domains.</li>
</ul>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" fetchpriority="high" decoding="async" class="alignnone wp-image-24278991" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/cadence.jpg?resize=481%2C356&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="481" height="356" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/cadence.jpg?resize=300%2C222&amp;ssl=1 300w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/cadence.jpg?resize=600%2C444&amp;ssl=1 600w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/cadence.jpg?w=624&amp;ssl=1 624w" sizes="(max-width: 481px) 100vw, 481px" /><br />
<strong>Fig. 1: Chip concept of a super agent for PCB and advanced packaging. Source: Cadence</strong></p>
<h3 role="heading" aria-level="2" data-uw-rm-heading="level"><strong>Financials</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li><a href="https://ir.aseglobal.com/html/ir_revenues.php">ASE</a>, <a href="https://www.asml.com/en/news/press-releases/2026/q2-2026-financial-results">ASML</a>, <a href="https://newsroom.ibm.com/2026-07-14-Arvind-Krishnas-Letter-to-IBM-Investors">IBM</a> (prelim), <a href="https://www.nanya.com/Content/InvestorCalendar/5722_20260710_Press%20Release_NTC%202Q26%20Financial%20Report.pdf">Nanya</a>, and <a href="https://pr.tsmc.com/english/news/3326">TSMC</a>.</li>
</ul>
<h3 role="heading" aria-level="2" data-uw-rm-heading="level"><strong>Around the valley</strong></h3>

<hr />
<h2 id="Global"><strong>Global</strong></h2>
<h3><strong>Americas</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Siemens EDA</strong> <a href="https://news.siemens.com/en-us/saskatoon-research-development-hub-2026/">expanded</a> its Saskatoon<strong>, Canada</strong> R&amp;D hub by 10K square feet for a total of 45K square feet.</li>
<li><strong>Meta</strong> is planning a <a href="https://about.fb.com/news/2026/07/teachers-local-businesses-win-as-meta-expands-louisiana-data-center/">$50B expansion</a> of its Richland Parish, <strong>Louisiana </strong>data center to 5GW of compute capacity, with teacher bonuses and local business contracts helping to minimize negative impacts.</li>
<li><strong>New York</strong> issued a <a href="https://www.governor.ny.gov/news/first-statewide-moratorium-new-hyperscale-data-centers-launched-governor-kathy-hochul">statewide moratorium</a> on new data centers of 50 MW or more, a one-year pause to ensure the state&#8217;s residents are not paying for transmission and infrastructure buildouts.</li>
<li>The <strong>U.S. NSF </strong>announced the newest <a href="https://www.nsf.gov/news/nsf-awards-12-new-regional-innovation-engines-fuel-research">Engines awards</a> to 12 teams across 20 states for critical applications such as semiconductors, energy grid security, critical mineral mining extraction, and advancing quantum computing.</li>
</ul>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone wp-image-24278992 size-full" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/map.png?resize=624%2C332&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="624" height="332" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/map.png?w=624&amp;ssl=1 624w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/map.png?resize=300%2C160&amp;ssl=1 300w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/map.png?resize=600%2C319&amp;ssl=1 600w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 624px) 100vw, 624px" /></p>
<p><strong>Fig. 3: A map showing the locations of the NSF</strong><strong> </strong><strong>Regional Innovation Engines cohorts 1 and 2. Source: NSF</strong></p>
<h3><strong>Europe</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li>The <strong>Barcelona Supercomputing Center</strong> opened the <a href="https://www.bsc.es/news/bsc-news/bsc-host-new-eurotpc-office-europe’s-strategic-voice-ai-science">EuroTPC office</a> as part of the Trillion Parameter Consortium to coordinate researchers, HPC/AI centers, and other players to build open, trustworthy AI for science.</li>
<li><strong>Keysight </strong>will lead a three‑year development program focused on creating <a href="https://www.keysight.com/us/en/about/newsroom/news-releases/2026/0715_pr26-050-keysight-and-sateliot-selected-by-european-space-agency-to-develop-blockchain-enabled-framework-for-5g-non-terrestrial-networks.html">secure, blockchain‑enabled anomaly detection</a> for 5G non‑terrestrial networks.</li>
<li>The <strong>Chips Joint Undertaking </strong>annual activity report 2025 is <a href="https://www.chips-ju.europa.eu/viewfile/?id=8333aacc-7f74-f111-ab0e-7c1e5287eb89">now available</a>.</li>
</ul>
<h3><strong>Asia</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>ASML</strong> plans to increase its <a href="https://www.asml.com/en/news/press-releases/2026/q2-2026-financial-results">low-NA EUV and DUV immersion</a> capacity by about 30% in 2027.</li>
<li><strong>TSMC</strong> broke ground on the second phase of Taiwan Chiayi Science Park, where <a href="https://www.reuters.com/world/asia-pacific/tsmc-add-2-advanced-chip-packaging-plants-chiayi-taiwan-minister-says-2026-07-13/">additional advanced packaging</a> plants are planned, as its first facility at the site ramps into mass production.</li>
<li><strong>Zeiss</strong> opened an innovation <a href="https://www.zeiss.com/semiconductor-manufacturing-technology/news-and-events/smt-press-releases/2026/innovation-center-korea.html">center</a> in Yongin, Korea.</li>
<li>China exported $177B worth of ICs during the first half of 2026, <a href="https://www.trendforce.com/news/2026/07/16/news-chinas-ic-export-value-nearly-doubles-to-us177b-in-1h26-memory-price-boom-seen-behind-the-surge">up 96% from a year earlier</a>, an increase analysts attributed partly to higher memory prices.</li>
<li>Shanghai developed a system to synergize <a href="https://www.scmp.com/economy/china-economy/article/3360801/shanghai-synergises-computing-centres-grid-power-infrastructure-upgrade">compute centers with the electric grid</a> to optimize peak load management, per SCMP.</li>
</ul>

<li><strong>Apple</strong> is reportedly looking to buy startups that make <a href="https://www.reuters.com/business/apple-chasing-ai-chip-company-deals-information-reports-2026-07-15/">server processors</a> for AI.</li>

</ul>
<hr />
<h2 id="New Technologies"><strong>New Technologies</strong></h2>
<p><strong>Advantest</strong> extended its <a href="https://www.advantest.com/en/news/2026/20260716.html">SiConic bench platform</a> into design-for-test engineering, allowing teams to execute, debug, and validate test patterns in a V93000-compatible environment before moving them onto production testers.</p>
<p><strong>Liquid Instruments</strong> announced GenInst Studio, which combines agentic AI with reconfigurable hardware to enable engineers to rapidly design and deploy <a href="https://liquidinstruments.com/news-updates/liquid-instruments-puts-first-ai-powered-instrument-creation-in-the-hands-of-every-engineer/">application-specific test instruments</a> in minutes rather than months.</p>
<p><strong>Whalechip</strong> successfully deployed <strong>ChipAgents</strong>&#8216; AI-powered design platform, enabling its engineering team to rapidly identify critical design issues in a new chip and significantly reduce the time required for <a href="https://chipagents.ai/newsroom">root cause analysis</a>.</p>
<p><strong>Infineon Technologies</strong> introduced a <a href="https://www.infineon.com/technology-news/2026/inftn202607-122">radiation-hardened GaN high-electron mobility transistor</a> driver designed for satellite and high-reliability space applications.</p>
<p><strong>ZeroPoint</strong>, a Swedish memory compression specialist, launched its ZeroStream hardware IP to <a href="https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/zeropoint-technologies-announces-zerostream-302826416.html">increase effective memory bandwidth</a>, delivering more tokens per second from AI accelerators.</p>
<p><strong>Chipmind</strong> released RTL Canvas, a <a href="https://www.chipmind.ai/post/chipmind-introduces-rtl-canvas-generative-ui-for-chip-design-agents">generative UI for chip design agents</a>, described as a bidirectional contract surface between chip design engineers and AI agents in which engineers sketch architectural intent directly on the RTL diagram.</p>
<p>AI opens the door to exploring a much larger solution space, similar to what high-level synthesis did years ago, but questions persist about the impact of increasing reliance on what is essentially a <a href="https://semiengineering.com/ai-in-chip-design-lots-of-promise-plenty-of-unanswered-questions/">black-box chip design</a>.</p>
<p><strong>Kandou AI</strong> licensed <strong>Baya Systems</strong>’ WeaveIP fabric and WeaverPro SW platform to help <a href="https://bayasystems.com/2026/07/14/kandou-ai-selects-baya-systems-to-power-attack-on-ai-memory-wall/">overcome the AI memory wall</a> with copper MIMO technology.</p>
<hr />
<h2 id="Research"><strong>Research</strong></h2>
<p><strong>KAIST</strong> researchers created a continuous semimetal–semiconductor junction within a single atomically thin PtSe₂ film, <a href="https://news.kaist.ac.kr/site/newsen/html/news/?mode=V&amp;mng_no=64330&amp;skey=&amp;sval=&amp;list_s_date=&amp;list_e_date=&amp;GotoPage=1">reducing the electrical resistance</a> typically introduced where separate metal contacts meet a semiconductor. AFM measurements directly visualized charge transport across the junction, pointing to a potential route to more efficient 2D transistors.</p>
<p><strong>UIUC, Argonne,</strong> and<strong> UC Colorado</strong> researchers created ultra-low-noise, tunable magnetic oscillations in <a href="https://matse.illinois.edu/news/87027">thin-film yttrium iron garnet</a>, enabling up to 40 dB amplification for low-power microwave and spintronic computing.</p>
<p>A <strong>CUHK</strong> team developed a closed-loop <a href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.12788">design framework for 3D-IC</a>s, connecting architectural simulation with physical block representation, thermal-aware floor-planning, cache behavior and sustained-frequency modeling.</p>
<p>An <strong>ETH Zurich</strong> team used &#8220;lightweight and practical ML methods to enable adaptive, <a href="https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/11413945">data-driven control throughout the memory hierarchy</a>,&#8221; demonstrating that &#8220;integrating adaptive learning into memory subsystems can lead to intelligent, self-optimizing architectures.&#8221;</p>
<p><strong>Imec</strong> developed a <a href="https://www.imec-int.com/en/articles/neural-telemetry-breakthrough-new-chip-delivers-ten-fold-compression-while-preserving">neuromorphic compressive telemetry chip</a> that reduces neural data volumes by more than 10x while preserving signal fidelity.</p>

<h2 id="Security"><strong>Security</strong></h2>
<h3><strong>Government on security</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li>The U.S. announced its new <a href="https://www.whitehouse.gov/releases/2026/07/white-house-launches-gold-eagle-initiative-for-unprecedented-cybersecurity-vulnerability-coordination/">Gold Eagle</a> initiative, a cyber vulnerability clearinghouse meant to support better industry-wide security collaboration in the face of growing AI threats.</li>
<li><strong>CISA</strong>,<strong> NSA</strong>, and international partners released guidance for software suppliers on <a href="https://www.cisa.gov/resources-tools/resources/establishing-coordinated-vulnerability-disclosure-program-work-security-researchers">establishing coordinated vulnerability-disclosure programs</a>.</li>
</ul>
<h3><strong>Hacker karma</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li>Two men who <a href="https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/c4gyg0y6yg2o">livestreamed their hack</a> of Transport for London when they were teenagers were sentenced to five years in prison. The hack disrupted service for months and reportedly cost the transit system £29M.</li>
</ul>
<h3><strong>Alerts, vulnerabilities</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>CISA</strong> warned that Russian FSB Center 16 cyber actors are <a href="https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa26-194a">exploiting poorly configured and end-of-life routers</a> to access critical-infrastructure networks. The cyber agency also posted <span draggable="true"><a href="https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">new vulnerability alerts</a></span> this week.</li>
<li><strong>Nvidia</strong> patched 12 TensorRT-LLM <a href="https://nvidia.custhelp.com/app/answers/detail/a_id/5840">vulnerabilities</a>, including a high-severity flaw that could enable local code execution and privilege escalation.</li>
<li><strong>Zoom</strong> released an update <a href="https://www.zoom.com/en/trust/security-bulletin/zsb-26014/">patching a vulnerability</a> that left Windows users open to account takeovers.</li>
</ul>
<h3 class="p1"><strong>Security papers and resources</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li class="p1">The complete <strong>Hardwear.io</strong> USA 2026 playlist is now available on YouTube, including technical talks and research from <a href="https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLWAG1Gbib488">hardware security</a> experts.</li>
<li><a href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.13939">PQC RISC-V eXtension Architecture</a> (<strong>Politecnico di Torino</strong> et al.)</li>
<li><a href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.10392?">Dynamic Rowhammer</a> Threshold Management (<strong>Santa Clara U</strong>.)</li>
<li><a href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.09473">Physical Fault Injection</a> in Embedded Neural Networks (<strong>Radboud U</strong>.)</li>
<li>Mechanized operational semantics of <a href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.10314">Rowhammer</a> (<strong>Sussex, Huawei</strong>.)</li>
<li><strong>The World Economic Forum</strong> published an analysis identifying the <a href="https://www.weforum.org/stories/cybersecurity/cybersecurity-digital-risk-fifa-world-cup/">FIFA World Cup as a cybersecurity case study</a>, with the sprawling international tournament showcasing the importance of ecosystem-wide security design.</li>
</ul>
<hr />
<h2 id="Vehicles, Batteries"><strong>Vehicles, Batteries</strong></h2>
<h3><strong>Chips for vehicles</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Micron</strong> signed long-term <a href="https://investors.micron.com/news-releases/news-release-details/micron-strengthens-automotive-ecosystem-supply-through-strategic?">memory and storage supply agreements</a> with Tier 1 automotive suppliers/partners, including Harman, Visteon, Denso, JOYNEXT, and Hyundai Mobis, to ensure memory supplies for autonomous driving, connectivity, and  SDV platforms.</li>
<li><strong>Iridium</strong> introduced its <a href="https://investor.iridium.com/2026-07-14-Iridium-Announces-Commercial-Availability-of-Iridium-PNT-ASIC,-Bringing-Resilient-GNSS-Protection-to-Devices-Worldwide">positioning, navigation, and timing ASIC</a>, designed to protect GPS- and GNSS-dependent devices from jamming, spoofing, and other growing threats.</li>
<li><strong>Nvidia </strong>auto head Xinzhou Wu told The Verge&#8217;s Decoder podcast that his team still has to <a href="https://www.theverge.com/podcast/964614/nvidia-auto-xinzhou-wu-ev-ai-hyperion-autonomy-cars-tesla">fight</a> for its share of the company&#8217;s compute resources amid an AI-driven surge in GPU demand.</li>
</ul>
<h3><strong>Autonomous</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li>Big screen innovator<strong> IMAX</strong> will partner with China&#8217;s <strong>Goer Dynamics</strong> to develop an immersive in-vehicle entertainment system for AVs, telling investors that the audio and video displays will help self-driving cars function as &#8220;<a href="https://investors.imax.com/news-releases/news-release-details/goer-dynamics-and-imax-partner-develop-worlds-first-imax-vehicle">third living spaces</a>.&#8221;</li>
<li>A <strong>Northwestern University</strong> team developed a drone capable of becoming <a href="https://news.northwestern.edu/stories/2026/07/new-spinning-drone-hides-in-plain-sight">nearly invisible</a> while flying. Called the “Phantom Twist,” the drone spins up to 25 times per second, which is too fast for the human eye to see clearly.</li>
</ul>
<h3><strong>EVs, batteries</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Gigafactory Malaysia</strong> will start small-scale production of the country&#8217;s first homegrown <a href="https://asia.nikkei.com/business/automobiles/electric-vehicles/malaysia-to-start-initial-production-of-homegrown-ev-battery-in-july">graphene-enhanced lithium-ion battery</a> for EVs this month.</li>
<li><strong>Century Lithium</strong> successfully converted lithium carbonate mined from southwest Nevada into <a href="https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/century-lithium-reports-lithium-metal-from-angel-island-demonstrated-in-battery-cells-under-us-army-sbir-program-302825745.html">lithium metal for battery cells</a>.</li>
<li><strong>Battery X Metals</strong> reports its patent-pending <a href="https://www.batteryxmetals.com/allnews/pressrelease10july2026?">battery rebalancing technology</a>, which restores usable EV battery capacity through cell balancing rather than battery replacement, is showing good preliminary results.</li>
<li>U.S. EV sales in Q2 were down 20.5% compared to last year. While sales improved from Q1 of this year, the quarter marked the third straight year-over-year backslide for U.S. EV sales, according to <a href="https://www.coxautoinc.com/insights/q2-2026-ev-sales-report-commentary/">Cox Automotive</a> analysis.</li>
</ul>

<li><a href="https://arxiv.org/pdf/2607.08137">Secur</a><a href="https://arxiv.org/pdf/2607.08137">ing AV Systems via Twin-Aware Federated RL</a> (<strong>NC State, Louisville</strong>)</li>

<h2 id="Trending Video"><strong>Trending Video</strong></h2>
<p><a href="https://semiengineering.com/how-far-left-can-you-shift/">How Far Left Can You Shift</a>? More steps in the design flow are shifting left, which makes a complicated design process even more complex. This includes early software prototyping, workload mapping, verification, multi-physics integration and IP qualification. Synopsys&#8217; <strong>Frank Schirrmeister</strong> talks about the increasing number of steps, the potential trouble spots, how reuse and chiplets impact the flow, and what engineers need to know in order to get a working chip out the door and on schedule.</p>
<div class="excerpt">
<p><iframe loading="lazy" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/zV6Zq-FTzzg" width="480" height="270" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen="allowfullscreen"></iframe></p>
</div>
<div class="row">
<div class="col-xs-10"></div>
</div>
<hr />
<h2 id="Quantum"><strong>Quantum</strong></h2>
<p>The <strong>EU</strong> launched Q-PLANET, a €50M <a href="https://www.pasqal.com/newsroom/launch-of-q-planet-european-pilot-line-to-industrialize-neutral-atom-quantum-chips/">European Quantum Chip Stability Pilot Line</a> coordinated by <strong>Pasqal</strong> to develop industrial-grade chip components for neutral atom quantum computing, sensing, and communication.</p>
<p>The <strong>European Space Agency</strong> is installing <strong>Equal1</strong>’s Bell-1 quantum computer, a 6-qubit system, and will research the benefits of <a href="https://philab.esa.int/esas-first-quantum-computer-will-shift-computing-frontiers-in-space/">hybrid classical-quantum</a> computing for complex Earth observation.</p>
<p><strong>QuiX Quantum</strong> launched Carina, a <a href="https://www.quixquantum.com/news/quix-quantum-announces-carina">universal photonic quantum computing architecture</a> for commercial deployment, developed for the <strong>German Aerospace Center</strong>.</p>
<p><strong>Photon Queue</strong> won a $500k grant from the <strong>New Mexico </strong>government and others to establish a local presence and support hiring, lab operations, and <a href="https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260714223804/en/Photon-Queue-Wins-%24500000-Grant-to-Establish-Quantum-Technology-Presence-in-New-Mexico">quantum device assembly</a>, test, and verification.</p>
<h3><strong>Quantum takes</strong></h3>
<ul>
<li>How to Win the <a href="https://www.hoover.org/research/how-win-quantum-race">Quantum Race</a> (<strong>Hoover</strong>)</li>
<li><a href="https://www.hoover.org/research/quantum-revolution-guide-allied-policymakers">Quantum Revolution</a>: Guide for Allied Policymakers (<strong>Hoover)</strong></li>
</ul>
<hr />
<h2 id="Workforce, Education"><strong>Workforce, Education</strong></h2>
<p><strong>New York</strong> rolled out an <a href="https://www.governor.ny.gov/news/governor-hochul-announces-launch-new-semiconductor-manufacturing-workforce-training-tax-credit">IC manufacturing workforce training tax-credit</a> program to help chip companies offset upskilling and apprenticeship costs, covering up to 75% of eligible training expenses as the state tries to build talent pipelines.</p>
<p><strong>University of Idaho</strong> students are creating a <a href="https://www.uidaho.edu/newsroom/semiconductor-education-vr-program">virtual-reality cleanroom simulator</a> to give students hands-on semiconductor manufacturing experience without requiring access to a physical cleanroom.</p>

		<item>
		<title>Why Metal TIM Warpage Simulations Fail—And How To Fix Them</title>
		<link>https://semiengineering.com/why-metal-tim-warpage-simulations-fail-and-how-to-fix-them/</link>
					<comments>https://semiengineering.com/why-metal-tim-warpage-simulations-fail-and-how-to-fix-them/#respond</comments>
		
		<dc:creator><![CDATA[Dambi Jo]]></dc:creator>
		<pubDate>Thu, 16 Jul 2026 07:08:58 +0000</pubDate>
				<category><![CDATA[Blogs - MD]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Chip, Package, System]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Manufacturing, Packaging and Materials]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[advanced packaging]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Amkor]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[characterization]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[packaging]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[thermal dissipation]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[thermal interface materials]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[TIMs]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[warpage]]></category>
		<guid isPermaLink="false">https://semiengineering.com/?p=24278928</guid>

					<description><![CDATA[<p>Characterizing the properties of thermal interface materials under actual application conditions. </p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/why-metal-tim-warpage-simulations-fail-and-how-to-fix-them/">Why Metal TIM Warpage Simulations Fail—And How To Fix Them</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></description>
										<content:encoded><![CDATA[<p><strong>By Dambi Jo, JiHyun Kim, GaHyeon Kim, TaeKyeong Hwang, WonChul Do, and KyungRok Park</strong></p>
<p>In the semiconductor packaging industry, it is common practice to predict warpage and stress levels through simulation prior to actual product fabrication. This approach helps minimize the cost and time associated with prototype production [1]. The need for simulation-driven design and material optimization has grown increasingly critical in recent years, as the cost of producing high-performance package prototypes has increased significantly. However, as packages become more advanced and materials more diverse, accurate prediction through simulation has become increasingly challenging, thereby highlighting the growing importance of simulation validation [2].</p>
<p>Recently, the demand for high-performance semiconductor packages—such as those used in artificial intelligence (AI), high performance computing (HPC), and automotive applications— has been growing rapidly. These advanced packages inherently generate significant heat during operation. Since the heat generated from the die directly affects package performance, efficient thermal dissipation is critically important. In response to this trend, thermal interface materials (TIMs) positioned between the die and the package have emerged as a key factor in thermal management [3]. Metal TIMs, which exhibit higher thermal conductivity than conventional polymer TIMs, are actively being adopted [4].</p>
<p>For packages utilizing polymer TIMs, mechanical behavior is well-characterized due to years of accumulated data, and simulation results tend to correlate well with actual behavior. In contrast, metal TIM packages are relatively new, and relevant data remains limited. Typically, simulations rely on material properties provided by suppliers, which are based on bulk specimens. However, applying these bulk-based properties directly to simulations often leads to discrepancies with actual package behavior. This is primarily because metal TIMs are measured in bulk form with high elastic modulus, whereas in real applications, they are implemented as thin films. Therefore, it is essential to characterize the material properties of metal TIMs under actual application conditions [2].</p>
<p>In this study, a sufficient number of metal TIM packages were evaluated using the shadow moiré technique to quantitatively analyze mechanical behavior across temperature variations [5]. During simulation, the material properties of the TIM were empirically adjusted to inductively derive values that closely match their actual behavior. To experimentally validate the simulation-derived properties, various material characterization techniques—including DMA, tensile testing, and rheometer—were conducted [6]. Through this process, the most suitable testing method showing the highest correlation with simulation results was identified.</p>
<p>The goal of this research is to establish a simulation model capable of accurately predicting the mechanical behavior of metal TIM packages and to support this model with experimental data. This will contribute to enhancing design reliability and reducing development costs and time in the fabrication of high-performance semiconductor packages.</p>
<h2>Characterization of mechanical behavior in metal TIM packages</h2>
<p>To investigate the mechanical behavior of metal TIM packages, a lidded flip chip ball grid array (FCBGA) test vehicle incorporating a metal TIM was fabricated and warpage measurements were conducted using a shadow moiré system.</p>
<h3>Preparation for lidded FCBGA test vehicle (Metal TIM vs. Polymer TIM)</h3>
<p>The metal TIM test vehicle (TV) used in this study includes a die with dimensions of 25.6 mm × 25.6 mm and a thickness of 0.55 mm. A hat-type lid with a thickness of 1.5 mm was assembled. The metal TIM was applied using a 0.4-mm thick indium 10 silver (In10Ag) preform. In the case of the polymer TIM test vehicle, all other conditions were kept identical, except for the die thickness and TIM material. The die thickness was 0.78 mm and a gel-type polymer TIM with a thickness of 0.07 mm was applied.</p>
<h3>Preparation for lidded FCBGA test vehicle (Metal TIM vs. Polymer TIM)</h3>
<p>The metal TIM test vehicle (TV) used in this study includes a die with dimensions of 25.6 mm × 25.6 mm and a thickness of 0.55 mm. A hat-type lid with a thickness of 1.5 mm was assembled. The metal TIM was applied using a 0.4-mm thick indium 10 silver (In10Ag) preform. In the case of the polymer TIM test vehicle, all other conditions were kept identical, except for the die thickness and TIM material. The die thickness was 0.78 mm and a gel-type polymer TIM with a thickness of 0.07 mm was applied.</p>
<h3>Shadow moiré test</h3>
<p>Warpage is the most used indicator for evaluating the mechanical behavior of semiconductor packages. In this study, warpage was characterized and quantified in accordance with the JEDEC JESD22-B112 standard [7]. To enable temperature measurements during testing, a dummy sample identical to the actual package was prepared and a thermocouple was attached to the bottom surface of the substrate. The dummy sample was placed at the center of the shadow moiré system, surrounded by actual packages. The temperature was increased from 25°C to 260°C at a rate of 5°C/min, and the warpage of the substrate&#8217;s bottom side was measured as a function of temperature. In addition to measurements on the final package, shadow moiré tests were also conducted after the underfill process to observe mechanical behavior changes due to processing.</p>
<h3>Mechanical behavior analysis: Polymer vs. metal TIM packages</h3>
<p>Shadow moiré measurements revealed that both polymer and metal TIM packages exhibited similar warpage behavior. As shown in Table 1, after the underfill process, the “crying mode&#8221; warpage was observed at room temperature, while &#8220;smile mode&#8221; warpage appeared at elevated temperatures. In the final package state, as shown in Table 2 and Table 3, both TIM types showed a &#8220;W&#8221;-shaped warpage at room temperature and an &#8220;M&#8221;-shaped warpage at high temperatures. Notably, both packages exhibited a relatively flat profile around 150°C, suggesting that this temperature can be considered the deformation-free temperature for simulation purposes.</p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278929" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-table1.webp?resize=529%2C382&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="529" height="382" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-table1.webp?w=529&amp;ssl=1 529w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-table1.webp?resize=300%2C217&amp;ssl=1 300w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 529px) 100vw, 529px" /></p>
<p><strong>Table 1: After Underfill Status shadow moiré results.</strong></p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278930" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-table2.webp?resize=594%2C555&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="594" height="555" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-table2.webp?w=594&amp;ssl=1 594w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-table2.webp?resize=300%2C280&amp;ssl=1 300w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 594px) 100vw, 594px" /></p>
<p><strong>Table 2: Metal TIM Package EOL Status shadow moiré results.</strong></p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278931" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-table3.webp?resize=579%2C555&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="579" height="555" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-table3.webp?w=579&amp;ssl=1 579w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-table3.webp?resize=300%2C288&amp;ssl=1 300w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-table3.webp?resize=24%2C24&amp;ssl=1 24w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 579px) 100vw, 579px" /></p>
<p><strong>Table 3: Polymer TIM Package EOL Status shadow moiré results.</strong></p>
<h2>Mechanical simulation</h2>
<p>In this study, mechanical simulations were conducted using material property data provided by the supplier. As shown in Table 4, the elastic modulus of the metal TIM was reported to be 15.5 gigapascals (GPa), which is significantly higher than that of the polymer TIM. All material configurations of the two packages were identical except for the TIM, and the material properties used in the simulation are listed in the table.</p>
<p>The simulation model was constructed using a quarter-symmetry structure, and all material properties were assumed to follow linear elastic behavior as a function of temperature. The substrate was modeled with a full-layered structure, and all bonding interfaces within the package were defined as perfectly bonded. Simulations were performed for different process conditions, and the corresponding models are illustrated in Fig. 1(a) and Fig. 1(b).</p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278932" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-table4.webp?resize=520%2C151&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="520" height="151" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-table4.webp?w=520&amp;ssl=1 520w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-table4.webp?resize=300%2C87&amp;ssl=1 300w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 520px) 100vw, 520px" /></p>
<p><strong>Table 4: Supplier provided TIM Material Property.</strong></p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278933" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-fig1.webp?resize=458%2C132&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="458" height="132" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-fig1.webp?w=458&amp;ssl=1 458w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-fig1.webp?resize=300%2C86&amp;ssl=1 300w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 458px) 100vw, 458px" /></p>
<p><strong>Fig. 1: Quarter symmetric Lidded FCBGA model, (a) After Underfill status, (b) EOL status.</strong></p>
<h3>Simulation results for the after-underfill condition</h3>
<p>Simulations were first conducted on the package in the after underfill condition, where the TIM, lid and lid adhesive were not yet applied. As shown in the model illustration of Table 5, the simulation results revealed a distinct crying mode warpage, which was consistent with the shadow moiré measurements. Simulations were performed across the entire temperature range used in the shadow moiré tests, and the results showed a high degree of agreement with the experimental data.</p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278934" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-table5.webp?resize=464%2C365&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="464" height="365" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-table5.webp?w=464&amp;ssl=1 464w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-table5.webp?resize=300%2C236&amp;ssl=1 300w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 464px) 100vw, 464px" /></p>
<p><strong>Table 5: After Underfill status simulation results.</strong></p>
<h3>Simulation results for the end-of-line (EOL) condition</h3>
<p>Simulation was performed on the final package configuration, which included the TIM, lid, and lid adhesive. As shown in Table 6, the metal TIM package exhibited a complete smile-mode warpage at room temperature. This result differs from the “W”-shaped warpage observed in the actual shadow moiré measurements. As illustrated in Fig. 2, the discrepancy between the simulation and experimental results is most pronounced in the room-temperature range.</p>
<p>In contrast, the polymer TIM package showed excellent agreement between simulation and experimental measurements, as confirmed in Table 7 and Fig. 3. Both the warpage values and shapes across the temperature range matched closely. Since all material properties except for the TIM were kept identical in the simulation, this discrepancy is presumed to originate from the material properties of the TIM itself.</p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278935" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-table6.webp?resize=464%2C388&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="464" height="388" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-table6.webp?w=464&amp;ssl=1 464w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-table6.webp?resize=300%2C251&amp;ssl=1 300w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 464px) 100vw, 464px" /></p>
<p><strong>Table 6: EOL status simulation result</strong><strong> – Metal TIM Package.</strong></p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278936" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-fig2.webp?resize=629%2C348&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="629" height="348" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-fig2.webp?w=629&amp;ssl=1 629w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-fig2.webp?resize=300%2C166&amp;ssl=1 300w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-fig2.webp?resize=600%2C332&amp;ssl=1 600w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 629px) 100vw, 629px" /></p>
<p><strong>Fig. 2: Simulated warpage of metal TIM package (EOL condition) using supplier-provided properties across temperature range.</strong></p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278937" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-table7.webp?resize=464%2C388&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="464" height="388" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-table7.webp?w=464&amp;ssl=1 464w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-table7.webp?resize=300%2C251&amp;ssl=1 300w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 464px) 100vw, 464px" /></p>
<p><strong>Table 7: EOL status simulation results</strong><strong> – Polymer TIM Package.</strong></p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278938" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-fig3.webp?resize=679%2C318&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="679" height="318" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-fig3.webp?w=679&amp;ssl=1 679w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-fig3.webp?resize=300%2C141&amp;ssl=1 300w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-fig3.webp?resize=600%2C281&amp;ssl=1 600w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 679px) 100vw, 679px" /></p>
<p><strong>Fig. 3: Simulated warpage of polymer TIM package (EOL condition) using supplier-provided properties across the temperature range.</strong></p>
<h2>Material characterization of metal TIM</h2>
<p>As shown in Fig. 4, the material properties of the metal TIM provided by the supplier were obtained through tensile testing using bulk dog-bone specimens. However, the metal TIM used in actual packages is a thin film with dimensions of 25 mm × 25 mm and a thickness of 0.4 mm, which differs significantly in geometry and thickness from the test specimens. Due to these differences, it was deemed inappropriate to directly apply the material properties measured from bulk specimens to simulation models.</p>
<p>To address this issue, this study conducted a series of material characterization tests on the film-type metal TIM used in actual packages.</p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278939" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-fig4.webp?resize=290%2C137&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="290" height="137" /></p>
<p><strong>Fig. 4: Supplier used bulk dog-bone specimen of metal TIM.</strong></p>
<h3>Film-type metal TIM characterization</h3>
<p>The film-type metal TIM used in the experiments was a thin film with dimensions of 25 mm × 25 mm and a thickness of 0.4 mm. Each specimen was processed according to the specific conditions required for the respective test methods. Four types of tests were conducted: tensile test, dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA), nano indentation, and rheometer.</p>
<p>The results from the tensile test, DMA, and nano indentation showed modulus values that were either close to or lower than the high values provided by the supplier. In contrast, the rheometer test yielded significantly lower modulus values. The data obtained through the rheometer can be seen in Fig. 5.</p>
<p>The key distinction of the rheometer test lies in its ability to incorporate process effects that occur during actual package fabrication. Unlike the other three methods, the rheometer test was designed to apply forces to the film-type metal TIM in a manner that closely mimics the conditions during package assembly. As shown in Fig. 6, the rheometer test applies force in both the top and bottom directions of the film, reflecting the nature of the process.</p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278940" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-fig5.webp?resize=442%2C261&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="442" height="261" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-fig5.webp?w=442&amp;ssl=1 442w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-fig5.webp?resize=300%2C177&amp;ssl=1 300w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 442px) 100vw, 442px" /></p>
<p><strong>Fig. 5: Rheometer result of film-type metal TIM.</strong></p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278941" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-fig6.webp?resize=254%2C463&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="254" height="463" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-fig6.webp?w=254&amp;ssl=1 254w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-fig6.webp?resize=165%2C300&amp;ssl=1 165w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 254px) 100vw, 254px" /></p>
<p><strong>Fig. 6: Schematic of the rheometer test setup for film-type metal TIM.</strong></p>
<h2>Correlation simulation study based on rheometer result</h2>
<p>The material characterization results confirmed that the modulus measured via rheometer testing was significantly lower than that obtained from bulk specimens. This rheometer-derived modulus was applied to the simulation to reproduce the warpage behavior of the metal TIM package.</p>
<p>As a result, the simulation using the rheometer-based modulus showed a high degree of agreement with the shadow moiré measurements, both in warpage shape and magnitude. Notably, as shown in Fig. 7 and Table 7, the simulation accurately captured not only the absolute warpage values across temperatures but also the shape transition from “W” to “M” mode.</p>
<p>These findings suggest that, for accurate simulation of metal TIM packages, material properties should be measured based on the actual film-type TIM used in the package rather than bulk specimens. Furthermore, the material data applied in simulations must reflect the directional forces and process effects encountered during real package assembly.</p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278942" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-fig7.webp?resize=614%2C280&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="614" height="280" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-fig7.webp?w=614&amp;ssl=1 614w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-fig7.webp?resize=300%2C137&amp;ssl=1 300w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-fig7.webp?resize=600%2C274&amp;ssl=1 600w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 614px) 100vw, 614px" /></p>
<p><strong>Fig. 7: Simulated warpage of the metal TIM package at EOL condition using rheometer-derived material properties across the temperature range.</strong></p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278943" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-table8.webp?resize=464%2C388&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="464" height="388" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-table8.webp?w=464&amp;ssl=1 464w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Amkor_Metal-TIM-Warpage-Sim-table8.webp?resize=300%2C251&amp;ssl=1 300w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 464px) 100vw, 464px" /></p>
<p><strong>Table 8: EOL status simulation results.</strong></p>
<h2>Conclusion</h2>
<p>In this study, the mechanical behavior of packages incorporating a metal TIM was quantitatively analyzed through shadow moiré testing, and warpage characteristics were evaluated by comparison with polymer TIM packages. The results confirmed that metal TIM packages exhibited similar warpage behavior to polymer TIM packages under both EOL and post-underfill conditions.</p>
<p>Initial simulation studies showed good agreement with shadow moiré measurements under post-underfill conditions. However, for metal TIM packages in the EOL state, the simulation results did not match the actual measurements. In contrast, the simulation results for polymer TIM packages at EOL showed strong correlation with experimental data, indicating that the accuracy of TIM material properties plays a critical role in simulation reliability.</p>
<p>To improve simulation accuracy, it was essential to obtain precise material properties that reflect the actual application conditions of the metal TIM. Therefore, instead of using bulk dog-bone specimens provided by suppliers, various material characterization tests were conducted on film-type metal TIM used in real packages. The results revealed that rheometer testing, which considers process-induced effects such as directional forces during assembly, yielded significantly lower modulus values compared to bulk specimens.</p>
<p>Applying the rheometer-derived modulus to the simulation resulted in a high level of agreement with shadow moiré measurements, both in warpage shape and magnitude. Notably, the simulation successfully reproduced the temperature-dependent warpage transition from “W” to “M” shape.</p>
<p>This study empirically demonstrates the limitations of using bulk specimen-based material properties in simulations and emphasizes the importance of acquiring material data that reflects both the actual specimen and the process effects encountered during package fabrication for metal TIMs. These findings provide a foundation for improving design reliability and effectively reducing prototype fabrication costs and development time in high-performance package development.</p>
<p>Future work will extend this research to include material characterization and simulation studies for various metal TIM compositions (e.g., In-Ag alloy, copper (Cu)-based), contributing to broader optimization in package design.</p>
<h3>References</h3>

		<title>From Feature-Scale Simulation To Digital Twins: Helping Process Engineers Tackle Growing Complexity</title>
		<link>https://semiengineering.com/from-feature-scale-simulation-to-digital-twins-helping-process-engineers-tackle-growing-complexity/</link>
					<comments>https://semiengineering.com/from-feature-scale-simulation-to-digital-twins-helping-process-engineers-tackle-growing-complexity/#respond</comments>
		
		<dc:creator><![CDATA[Saurabh Suryavanshi]]></dc:creator>
		<pubDate>Thu, 16 Jul 2026 07:06:16 +0000</pubDate>
				<category><![CDATA[Blogs - MD]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Manufacturing Solutions]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Manufacturing, Packaging and Materials]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[AI]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[ALE]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[atomic layer etching]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[digital twins]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[etch]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[GAA FETs]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[gate all around]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[physics simulation]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[process modeling]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[simulation]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Synopsys]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[TCAD]]></category>
		<guid isPermaLink="false">https://semiengineering.com/?p=24278952</guid>

					<description><![CDATA[<p>Physics-grounded workflows help process teams to explore, understand, and act on a rapidly expanding innovation space.</p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/from-feature-scale-simulation-to-digital-twins-helping-process-engineers-tackle-growing-complexity/">From Feature-Scale Simulation To Digital Twins: Helping Process Engineers Tackle Growing Complexity</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></description>
										<content:encoded><![CDATA[<p>For process engineers facing rising manufacturing complexity, physics-based simulation provides the foundation for understanding what is happening on the wafer. AI, automation, and digital twins become powerful only when they build on that foundation, enabling faster exploration without losing trust, calibration, or engineering judgment.</p>
<h2>The growing challenge of semiconductor manufacturing</h2>
<p>Semiconductor manufacturing has always been complex, but that complexity is compounding. Advanced nodes bring more etch-intensive integration schemes, tighter atomic-scale requirements, new materials, narrower process windows, and stronger coupling among plasma conditions, reaction chemistry, transport, and evolving geometry. Process teams must move faster with fewer opportunities for trial-and-error learning.</p>
<p>Feature-scale effects such as high-aspect-ratio etching, microtrenching, local loading, ion shadowing, redeposition, and material-dependent reactions now directly determine profile control, selectivity, critical dimension evolution, and process margin.</p>
<p>The pressure is especially visible in etch, where equipment spending is projected to rise from the mid-$20B range today toward nearly $40B by 2031, signaling fabs’ investment in more specialized etch capability as requirements become more demanding.</p>
<p>The requirements per step are also tightening. In advanced structures, removing or preserving only a few atomic layers can determine whether profile, selectivity, channel geometry, or device performance remains within margin. Gate-all-around architectures make this tangible because nanosheet release, residual SiGe, silicon loss, inner-spacer control, and lateral etch behavior must be balanced simultaneously.</p>
<p>The number of process knobs is also expanding. Cryogenic etch, atomic layer etching, pulsed plasma, selective chemistries, and advanced chamber controls expand the solution space while increasing the choices engineers must reason through under aggressive yield and performance timelines.</p>
<p>Traditional wafer-based experimentation is beginning to show its limits because process complexity is growing faster than expert capacity. Even as teams accumulate experience, the number of process interactions, device contexts, and candidate recipes can outpace the human ability to reason through them.</p>
<p>The industry is reaching a point where the bottleneck is not only hardware capability, but also the ability of process teams to explore, understand, and act on a rapidly expanding innovation space.</p>
<p>The implication is like software productivity shifts: when human effort becomes the limiting factor, workflows must move toward higher-level abstraction and automation. In process development, the answer is not simply more wafer splits or manual review, but physics-grounded workflows that help experts explore more possibilities.</p>
<h2>Why physics-based simulation is foundational</h2>
<p>To address this challenge, process engineers increasingly rely on physics-based simulation to translate fab observations and process conditions into feature-scale wafer outcomes. Feature-scale simulation provides a mechanistic framework for modeling plasma-surface interactions, transport and charging effects in deep 3D structures, and profile evolution during etch or deposition.</p>
<p>Feature-scale simulation connects <strong>equipment-level inputs</strong> such as power, pressure, and gas chemistry to <strong>feature-level results</strong> such as profile shape, depth, selectivity, and uniformity. Tools such as Sentaurus Topography provide the physics-based foundation for understanding, predicting, and optimizing complex process steps.</p>
<p>Figure 1 illustrates how Sentaurus Topography supports physics-based analysis of gate-all-around inner-spacer formation by modeling species transport, etch and deposition behavior, SiGe-to-silicon selectivity, and high-aspect-ratio cavity effects. Even a simplified structure shows how tightly coupled modules and process steps can affect source/drain epitaxy quality, underscoring why feature-scale physics must come before virtual optimization or AI-assisted exploration.</p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278953" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Feature-Scale-Sim-to-Digital-Twins-fig1.webp?resize=611%2C294&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="611" height="294" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Feature-Scale-Sim-to-Digital-Twins-fig1.webp?w=611&amp;ssl=1 611w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Feature-Scale-Sim-to-Digital-Twins-fig1.webp?resize=300%2C144&amp;ssl=1 300w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Feature-Scale-Sim-to-Digital-Twins-fig1.webp?resize=600%2C289&amp;ssl=1 600w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 611px) 100vw, 611px" /></p>
<p><strong>Fig. 1: Gate-all-around inner-spacer analysis with Sentaurus Topography.</strong></p>
<p>For Sentaurus Topography, key physics-based differentiators include feature-scale geometry evolution, surface reaction modeling, ion and neutral transport, charging-aware behavior, material-dependent selectivity, and profile prediction across complex 3D structures. Importantly, Sentaurus Topography can be calibrated to experimental data, helping models reproduce new experimental results more accurately. These capabilities make the digital twin more than a data-fitting layer; they provide the physical anchor that allows AI and automation to generalize beyond experiments already performed.</p>
<p>Figure 2 broadens this proof point by showing how the same physics foundation applies across high-value feature-scale applications, including deposition, etch, spin-on, CMP, loading effects, ion-induced surface charging, and doping-dependent etching. By connecting process conditions to wafer outcomes through calibrated physical models, Sentaurus Topography helps make digital twin and AI-enabled workflows more trustworthy.</p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278954" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Feature-Scale-Sim-to-Digital-Twins-fig2.webp?resize=619%2C305&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="619" height="305" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Feature-Scale-Sim-to-Digital-Twins-fig2.webp?w=619&amp;ssl=1 619w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Feature-Scale-Sim-to-Digital-Twins-fig2.webp?resize=300%2C148&amp;ssl=1 300w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Synopsys_Feature-Scale-Sim-to-Digital-Twins-fig2.webp?resize=600%2C296&amp;ssl=1 600w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 619px) 100vw, 619px" /></p>
<p><strong>Fig. 2: </strong><strong>Physics-based modeling across critical process applications with Sentaurus Topography.</strong></p>
<p>With this foundation, engineers can move beyond empirical iteration to a physics-grounded view of why outcomes occur, which parameters matter, and how the process can be improved.</p>
<p>This is also why AI alone is not enough. Empirical data remains constrained by wafer availability, metrology cost, and the practical limits of how many physical experiments can be run. Physics-based simulation can help expand the searchable innovation space by adding mechanistic structure where data is sparse, but its results still require calibration, validation, and expert judgment before they can guide wafer learning cycles.</p>
<p>Once physics-based simulation establishes a trusted foundation, the next question is scale: how can engineers create, calibrate, and explore models fast enough to keep up with complexity? Workflow automation helps experts apply that foundation across more mechanisms, recipes, and process conditions than manual methods can support.</p>
<h2>The next bottleneck: Model creation and exploration</h2>
<p>As simulation capabilities mature, the bottleneck shifts from execution to model construction: can engineers efficiently define reaction mechanisms, parameter sets, boundary conditions, and calibration strategies that represent the underlying process physics?</p>
<p>Creating accurate surface reaction models remains iterative, expert driven, and computationally demanding because engineers must translate incomplete observations into physically plausible mechanisms, tune parameters, and evaluate competing model structures.</p>
<p>Experts still spend significant time configuring models, perturbing parameters, running trial simulations, and comparing candidate solutions. The next layer of innovation is workflow automation that reduces repetitive setup while keeping experts focused on physical insight, validation, and judgment.</p>
<h2>Adding automation to the expert workflow</h2>
<p>To scale model development, the industry is beginning to automate parts of the modeling workflow while keeping experts responsible for assumptions, validation, and judging whether automated outputs are physically credible.</p>
<p>New approaches are emerging in which expert process knowledge is captured in structured model templates, multiple candidate mechanisms are generated automatically, and simulations are evaluated in parallel so engineers can concentrate on physical validation, model selection, and refinement.</p>
<p>This shifts the workflow from serial trial and error to guided parallel exploration, allowing engineers to assess multiple model possibilities without manually constructing every candidate. Automation becomes a <strong>force multiplier for expertise</strong> while keeping engineers responsible for validation and judgment.</p>
<h2>From simulation to digital twin</h2>
<p>As simulation and automation evolve, individual tools are giving way to integrated digital twin platforms that make physics-grounded decision-making part of everyday process engineering.</p>
<p>A process digital twin brings physics-based simulation, equipment parameters, process knowledge, and manufacturing data into a unified environment usable by process engineers, not only simulation specialists. It lets engineers evaluate virtual process changes, explore what-if scenarios before wafer runs, and connect equipment settings to wafer outcomes without adding manual complexity.</p>
<p>The emphasis on physics-grounded digital twin matters because AI capabilities are becoming broadly available. Many entrants can claim faster model generation, automated recipe search, or AI-assisted analysis. What is harder to replicate is validated process physics, feature-scale simulation, calibrated manufacturing context, and workflows that connect virtual predictions back to real wafer behavior.</p>
<h2>The role of AI, ML, and emerging agentic workflows</h2>
<p>As these systems evolve further, AI is beginning to play a more prominent role, but its value is strongest when it is grounded in semiconductor physics. ML can accelerate calibration and optimization, AI-driven methods can search high-dimensional parameter spaces and identify latent patterns, and agent-based approaches can help orchestrate complex simulation workflows. The differentiator is not AI by itself; it is AI working on top of validated physics models, process knowledge, and digital twin workflows that reflect how semiconductor manufacturing behaves.</p>
<p>Looking ahead, engineers may work with intelligent assistants that configure experiments, suggest model refinements, interpret results, and navigate workflows across models, data, and compute. The goal is not to abstract away physics or remove the engineer, but to put physics knowledge at the center of process engineering and use AI as the interface and acceleration layer.</p>
<h2>Helping engineers work at the speed of complexity</h2>
<p>Semiconductor manufacturing complexity is not slowing down. To keep pace, process engineers need workflows that combine physics-based simulation for mechanistic understanding, AI for faster model development, digital twins for decision support, and intelligent assistants to reduce workflow burden.</p>
<p>The future of process development lies at the intersection of physics, AI, and digital twin technologies: physics provides the trusted foundation, AI accelerates exploration, and digital twins make that capability usable in everyday workflows. This combination helps process engineers get more value from complex manufacturing equipment while preserving the physics knowledge needed to make virtual process decisions credible.</p>
<p>Learn more about Synopsys TCAD <a href="https://www.synopsys.com/manufacturing/tcad.html">here</a>.</p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/from-feature-scale-simulation-to-digital-twins-helping-process-engineers-tackle-growing-complexity/">From Feature-Scale Simulation To Digital Twins: Helping Process Engineers Tackle Growing Complexity</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></content:encoded>
